one_line_takeaway: By exploiting the render-parse gap in PDFs, INTEGRITY SHIELD's schema-aware document-layer watermarks (hidden text, CMap glyph remapping, off-page overlays) block black-box MLLMs from correctly answering shielded exam PDFs 91-94% of the time while remaining visually unchanged, and recover a consistent, attributable watermark signature from model outputs 89-93% of the time, outperforming prompt-only (ICW) and prior document-perturbation baselines (code-glyph, TRAPDOC) that either barely work or fail to transfer across models (e.g., TRAPDOC drops to 40.5% prevention / 43.4% detection on Gemini).
key_finding: On Prevention-ASR (Table 1), ICW is nearly ineffective (single digits: GPT 7.20%, Claude 5.80%, Grok 4.10%, Gemini 3.50%), confirming that prompt-only steering fails against black-box PDF ingestion. code-glyph reaches 86.30/84.70/83.20/81.90% (GPT/Claude/Grok/Gemini) and TRAPDOC reaches 88.70/86.40/85.10% on GPT/Claude/Grok but collapses to 40.50% on Gemini, indicating poor cross-model transfer. IeS-v1 achieves 91.20/90.80/90.50/90.10% and IeS-v2 achieves 93.60/92.90/92.30/91.70% across GPT/Claude/Grok/Gemini respectively -- consistently high and stable across all four models. Detection scores follow the same pattern: ICW is negligible (6.80/5.30/4.60/3.20%), code-glyph reaches the mid-80s (85.90/84.20/82.70/81.40%), TRAPDOC again drops on Gemini (87.90/85.80/84.60% on GPT/Claude/Grok vs. 43.40% on Gemini), while IeS-v1 (90.70/90.30/89.90/89.50%) and IeS-v2 (92.80/92.10/91.60/91.00%) remain in the high-80s/low-90s on all four models. Per-question-type breakdown (Table 2) shows unwatermarked accuracy of 94-97% across MCQ/T-F/LF for all models, collapsing to 3-8% residual accuracy under watermarking (an 85-90 point drop), with Long-Form questions showing the largest reductions for GPT and Claude. A qualitative case study (Table 3, an OSI-model routing question) shows all three baselines converge on the same wrong answer 'A' across all four models (no attributable signal), whereas IeS-v1 drives all models to distractor 'B' and IeS-v2 to distractor 'C', giving a distinguishable, method-specific watermark signature usable for attribution.
core_contribution: The paper introduces INTEGRITY SHIELD, a document-layer (PDF-substrate) watermarking system for exam/assessment PDFs that exploits the render-parse gap between what humans see and what MLLM PDF parsers ingest. It embeds schema-aware, item-level watermarks (invisible text spans anchored near stems/options, CMap-based glyph remapping so visually identical tokens parse differently, and clipped/off-page overlays with distractor cues) generated by an LLM-driven planner that assigns a watermark tactic per item based on question type (MCQ, True/False, Long-Form), gold answer, and layout metadata, while a PDF-rewriting service enforces that the rendered/visible appearance stays unchanged (verified against Adobe Reader, Chrome, macOS Preview). Two configurations are instantiated: IeS-v1 (lighter hidden-text + minimal glyph remapping) and IeS-v2 (denser multi-layer perturbations). The system serves two purposes simultaneously: prevention (blocking an MLLM from producing a correct/usable answer when a student uploads the shielded PDF) and detection (embedding a recoverable, item-level watermark signature so that if a model or student response does follow the induced distractor/keyphrase pattern, this can be attributed as AI-generated authorship evidence). The paper also ships an interactive demo/web interface for instructors to upload exams, preview watermark behavior, and inspect pre/post AI performance and authorship reports.
threat_model: The adversary is a student who uploads an instructor-provided, unmodified-looking exam PDF directly to a proprietary black-box MLLM (GPT-5, Claude Sonnet-4.5, Grok-4.1, Gemini-2.5 Flash accessed via official APIs) to obtain answers, bypassing existing defenses. The paper explicitly frames prior defenses as inadequate: post-hoc authorship/style classifiers and perplexity-based detectors (e.g., DetectGPT) only analyze the student's output and fail on short answers, paraphrasing, code, and mixed authorship; surveillance-based proctoring (keystroke/browser/gaze monitoring) is invasive and raises privacy/equity concerns; and decoder-level watermarking (e.g., Kirchenbauer et al.'s green-list watermark, in-context watermarking/ICW) requires control over the model's generation process, which is infeasible when students query proprietary black-box systems. INTEGRITY SHIELD instead instruments the document itself (the instructor's PDF) rather than the model or the student's output, assuming institutional control over the assessment PDF. The defender is the instructor/institution; the paper explicitly disclaims that authorship scores constitute proof of misconduct, positioning the system as a triage/interpretability aid feeding into human follow-up (oral checks, additional assessment) rather than an automated sanctioning system.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: evaluation is confined to a ten-exam benchmark and a fixed set of frontier MLLMs under simulated usage (models directly consuming instructor PDFs), which may not capture broader real-world variation in domains, languages, accessibility workflows (e.g., screen readers), or institution-specific formats; watermark robustness may drift as MLLM PDF-parsing pipelines evolve, requiring periodic re-calibration; the system is explicitly not a definitive misconduct detector -- authorship scores indicate alignment with a watermark signature, not proof of a policy violation, and are intended only as a triage signal for human follow-up, not automatic evidence for sanctions; the approach assumes institutional control over the assessment PDF, and the authors flag that similar techniques could be misapplied to non-assessment documents, restricting intended use to formal educational settings with governance and transparency policies. Reviewer-observed additional limitations grounded in the text: the paper does not report a robustness evaluation against adaptive/informed adversaries (e.g., a student or tool that strips invisible text/CMap remappings, re-OCRs the PDF, or otherwise pre-processes the file before upload); Table 1's own numbers show that even IeS-v2's strongest configuration leaves 6-8% of exams unprevented and 7-9% of items undetected per model, meaning failure is nontrivial at scale; the paper itself contains an unresolved internal discrepancy between '30 exams' (Introduction) and 'ten-exam benchmark' (contributions bullet, Sec 4.1 'Benchmark dataset' framing, and Limitations).

datasets_used: Self-compiled benchmark of exam-style PDFs web-scraped from public university course websites (e.g., Stanford and others), spanning mathematics, science, programming, humanities, and medical reasoning; ~10% of a larger scraped pool sampled to form the benchmark under criteria of >=10 questions, >=3 question formats, and clean PDF rendering; described inconsistently in the paper as both '30 exams' and a 'ten-exam benchmark'
quantitative_results: Per-question-type accuracy without watermark: 94-97% across MCQ/T-F/LF for all four models
Per-question-type accuracy with watermark (residual): 3-8% across MCQ/T-F/LF for all four models, an 85-90 point drop; Long-Form shows largest drop for GPT and Claude
Case study (OSI routing question, gold answer B): baselines (ICW, code-glyph, TRAPDOC) all converge to wrong answer A across all 4 models; IeS-v1 drives all models to B; IeS-v2 drives all models to C
baselines_compared: ICW (in-context watermarking via invisible small white-font prompt-side text; Liu et al. 2025)
code-glyph (bitcode-to-glyph mapping manipulation on question text; Xiong et al. 2025)
TRAPDOC (phantom-token/layout-trick document perturbations; Jin et al. 2025)
